content: Indemnification clauses should be mutual and capped at a defined limit (e.g., total contract value). One-sided indemnification that holds only one party harmless for all claims is considered highly risky. Best practice: require both parties to indemnify each other for their own negligence only, and exclude indirect/consequential damages from indemnity scope.

A 'hold harmless' clause transfers liability from one party to another. Broad hold harmless clauses that cover all claims including third-party actions and gross negligence should be avoided or negotiated down. Always ensure insurance requirements are tied to hold harmless obligations.

Limitation of liability clauses cap the maximum amount one party can recover from the other. The cap should be proportional to the contract value — typically equal to fees paid in the prior 12 months. Caps that are too low (e.g., $100) are almost certainly unenforceable. Exclusions for fraud, willful misconduct, death/personal injury, and IP infringement are standard.

Gross negligence represents a higher threshold than ordinary negligence, involving reckless disregard. Clauses that limit liability only for gross negligence (not ordinary negligence) shift significant risk to the non-drafting party. Ensure gross negligence and willful misconduct are always excluded from any liability cap or indemnification carve-out.
